title: Anger At Your Niceness As A Survival Strategy
dek: You’ve been taught that your niceness is a virtue, a shield against chaos and conflict. But what if beneath that polite exterior, your body is quietly seething? Your anger isn’t a failure; it’s a vital messenger, revealing how your niceness has become a survival tactic that tightens your jaw, knots your diaphragm, and silences your truth.
bodyMarkdown:
It’s 2 a.m., and the house breathes low and slow around you - soft hum of the refrigerator, the faint rustle of trees scraping the windowpane in a damp spring rain. Your body is awake in ways you don’t want it to be, a coil of tension twisting just beneath the surface. The masseter muscles along your jaw clamp down hard, teeth grinding invisibly in the dark. Your trapezius and levator scapulae, those twin sentinels guarding the base of your neck and shoulders, are tightened into knots that feel like they were tied by someone else’s hands. Your diaphragm, the great unseen breath-maker, is held shallow and tight as if your ribs are wrapped in invisible steel bands, preventing the full rise and fall of your lungs. Somewhere deep behind the sharp ache in your lower back, the psoas - the muscle often called the “muscle of survival” - seems clenched and unyielding, a secret fortress no one asked you to build but you did anyway.


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.167Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

This is the body of your niceness.

You’ve learned early, maybe before you even had words for it, that niceness is safety. It’s the quiet nod, the careful smile, the “yes” when you want to say “no,” the softening of your voice even as your insides bristle. You learned that your fury was ugly, dangerous, something to hide or smother. Niceness became your survival strategy, a way to keep the edges of your being from tearing open in a world that didn’t want to see your rage. But niceness is not just a social mask - it is a lived, somatic contract your body makes with the world, a holding pattern that comes with a physical toll.

Karla McLaren, a pioneering voice in the study of emotions, reminds us that anger is not a problem to be fixed but a vital energy to be understood. When you make niceness your default, you aren’t simply choosing politeness - you’re pushing anger underground, into the dark places of the body where it waits and tightens like a coiled spring. McLaren’s work shows that this repression doesn’t erase anger; it transforms it into tension, anxiety, and sometimes outright pain. The masseter muscle, for instance, is often the first to betray this hidden storm - your jaw clenches reflexively in response to threat, even if the threat is only imagined or internalized. This locking down of the jaw is a silent scream, a bracing for impact in a body that has been trained to contain its fury.

Bessel van der Kolk, whose groundbreaking work with trauma survivors reveals how deeply our bodies remember what our minds try to forget, points out that these physical patterns are not mere habits but survival imprints. The psoas muscle, which connects the lumbar spine to the femur, is central in this story. It’s the muscle that prepares you to run or freeze, to fight or flee. When you habitually choose niceness over anger, the psoas often stays chronically contracted, holding you in a state of low-grade defense. You may not realize how much your body is bracing all the time, but the tightness in your lower back, the difficulty in fully standing up straight, the shallow breathing - all these are signs that your body is still ready to protect you, even when the danger has long passed.

Niceness demands a certain posture. Your trapezius and levator scapulae muscles pull your shoulders up and forward, a subtle bowing of your body that signals submission and appeasement. This posture is familiar to anyone who grew up learning that their anger was unacceptable. You hold yourself small, curling in on your own edges to avoid rocking the boat. But this posture also compresses the diaphragm, restricting breath and silencing the voice. The diaphragm is the engine of speech, of emotional expression, and when it is tight and constricted, your ability to speak your truth is compromised. The breath becomes shallow, quick, and disconnected from the rest of your body. You may notice this most on restless nights like this one, when the quiet hum of the house becomes a loud reminder of the dissonance you carry inside.

The smell of rain on the windowsill mingles with the faint scent of chamomile from the tea you drank hours ago, a small ritual meant to soothe but somehow failing tonight. The body remembers what the mind tries to forget, and the memory is written deep in your fascia, muscles, and bones. You can feel it in the tight band across your forehead, the ache behind your eyes, the restless shift of your hips as the psoas rebels against the stillness. This is not a failure of will or character; this is biology. The body knows what it has endured, even when you have not yet found the words to say it.

McLaren teaches that anger is an energy that wants to move, to clear space, to set boundaries. When that energy is diverted into niceness, it becomes stuck, trapped in the very muscles designed to protect you. The masseter clenches, the trapezius contracts, the diaphragm tightens, and the psoas braces - all in a desperate attempt to hold you safe in a world that told you your anger was dangerous.

Van der Kolk’s work with trauma survivors reveals something crucial here: the body’s memory of safety or threat is not just metaphorical - it’s visceral. The nervous system does not forget the lessons of survival, even when the mind wants to believe otherwise. Your body’s posture of niceness is a map of where you have been, a living archive of what you once needed to survive. But the cost is high. Chronic muscle tension leads to pain, fatigue, and a sense of disconnection from yourself. The very strategy that once protected you now confines you.

Tonight, in the quiet hours when the world is asleep, you sit with this tension. You notice the clench of your jaw, the rigid line of your shoulders, the shallow catch of your breath. You are not alone. Women across the world carry this same burden, the weight of anger denied and niceness enforced. This is the first step toward a deeper reckoning with anger - not as a flaw, but as a messenger.

The rain taps steadily against the window, a steady rhythm that reminds you of the pulse in your own body, waiting to be heard. The next part of this exploration will invite you to listen more closely, to feel the edges of your fury without shame, and to begin the delicate work of unwinding the survival patterns that have held you in niceness for so long.

It’s 2:17 a.m., and the house still breathes with you, though now the rain has softened to a steady drizzle, the kind that blurs the edges of the world outside your window. Your body hums beneath your skin in ways that feel both familiar and strange - like an old song played in a minor key, one you know by heart but haven’t quite learned to listen to properly. Your masseter muscles remain clenched, a stubborn line of tension running along your jaw. The trapezius and levator scapulae still hold a quiet siege at the base of your neck, their tautness a physical punctuation mark to your story of niceness and survival.

But tonight, as you sit with this tension, you begin to consider another way forward - one that doesn’t ask you to will yourself out of the coil but to invite the body’s own wisdom in through herbs, movement, and somatic awareness. The body remembers, yes, but it also responds.

Resmaa Menakem, a somatic therapist and author of *My Grandmother’s Hands*, insists that racialized trauma is held in the body and that the healing of that trauma begins in the muscles and fascia. His work reminds us that trauma, including the trauma of enforced niceness, is not simply a psychological event but a physical imprint. He teaches that the body doesn’t just hold tension as a symptom; it holds it as a message. The muscles, the fascia, the nervous system - they all speak in a language that’s felt rather than thought. Menakem encourages us to start with the body’s edges - feet, hands, jaw - those places that carry the earliest signs of tension and trauma. This is where the healing begins, by listening and responding rather than resisting.

Robert Augustus Masters, whose work in embodied psychotherapy and the somatic experience of shame and rage, offers a complementary map. He points out that rage is “an energy that wants to move,” but when it’s held in the body, it turns to rigid patterns of contraction and suppression. Masters emphasizes the importance of “bearing witness” to this energy in the body - allowing the muscles to soften, the breath to deepen, and the nervous system to learn safety again. He calls this process “somatic witnessing,” a practice of compassionate attention to the lived experience of the body’s protective patterns. This is not about forcing change but about inviting the nervous system to relax its guard, muscle by muscle.

Herbs enter this conversation as allies - medicine made from the earth to support the nervous system’s delicate work of unwinding. Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) has long recognized the body’s subtle energetic patterns, identifying herbs that calm the Shen (spirit) and soothe the Liver, the organ system most closely associated with anger and its free flow. In TCM, anger is said to “rise,” often manifesting as tension in the head and neck or a feeling of heat under the ribs. When anger is suppressed, the Liver Qi stagnates, leading to physical symptoms that echo what you know too well: tight jaw, stiff neck, shallow breath.

Skullcap (*Scutellaria lateriflora*), a revered herb in both Western and Chinese traditions, is a powerful nervine tonic. It gently relaxes the nervous system, easing the masseter’s stubborn grip and allowing the diaphragm to expand more fully. In TCM, skullcap is considered cooling and calming, clearing heat and calming the spirit. Its flavonoids have sedative and anxiolytic properties that support the nervous system’s shift from fight-or-flight to rest-and-digest.

Passionflower (*Passiflora incarnata*) works in harmony with skullcap, offering a delicate but persistent invitation to relax. It’s a vine that climbs and unfolds slowly, much like the process of unwinding your own muscular armor. Passionflower supports GABA receptors in the brain, promoting calm and reducing the restless energy that tightens your trapezius and levator scapulae. It’s the kind of medicine that meets you where you are - never forcing but gently coaxing your body toward ease.

Dandelion root (*Taraxacum officinale*) might seem an unlikely companion in this trio, but it plays a vital role in supporting the Liver’s detoxification and the smooth flow of Qi. In TCM, dandelion clears heat and disperses stagnation. Physically, it supports the digestion and elimination pathways, which are often sluggish when the nervous system is overwhelmed by suppressed anger. The bitterness of dandelion root tea can be grounding, reminding you of the body’s need to release what no longer serves.

These herbs, taken as thoughtfully brewed teas or tinctures, become part of a ritual that honors your body’s need for safety and movement. They are not quick fixes but steady companions on the path of somatic healing. The chamomile you drank earlier was a start - a floral lullaby to soothe your tension. Tonight, adding skullcap, passionflower, and dandelion root to your bedside table might rewrite the story your muscles have been telling in silence.

Let’s try a simple embodied practice, one inspired by Menakem’s grounding techniques and Masters’ somatic witnessing. It requires no more than your breath, your attention, and the willingness to feel what’s present.

Sit quietly, feet flat on the floor, your hands resting gently on your thighs. Close your eyes if it feels safe to do so. Begin by noticing the weight of your feet pressing down into the ground. Feel the edges of your soles, the curve of your arches. As you breathe in, imagine the breath traveling down through your legs and pooling in your feet. As you breathe out, feel your feet soften into the floor, releasing any tightness.

Shift your attention slowly upward to your jaw. Notice the masseter muscles - the hard line along your cheeks and back toward your ears. Without judgment, simply observe any tightness or clenching. On your next exhale, allow your jaw to soften just a fraction. Let the teeth part slightly, the tongue rest comfortably behind the bottom front teeth. This is the body’s way of saying it’s ready to let go.

Now bring your hands up to your trapezius and levator scapulae muscles at the base of your neck and shoulders. Place your fingers gently on that taut ridge of muscle. Breathe in deeply, imagining the breath flowing into those tight spots. On the exhale, invite the muscles to relax even if it’s just a tiny release. Repeat this slow, deliberate attention three to five times.

Finally, return to your diaphragm and belly. Place one hand on your chest and the other on your belly. Notice how shallow or deep your breath is. With each inhale, imagine filling your lungs fully, expanding the diaphragm downward and outward. On each exhale, feel the breath release fully, softening the ribs and belly. This deep, diaphragmatic breathing signals safety to your nervous system, encouraging it to lower its guard.

As you open your eyes, you may notice a subtle shift - a small space between the clenched muscles, a softening of the body’s armor. This is the beginning of a conversation between your nervous system and your anger, a dialogue held in breath and muscle rather than words and willpower.

Menakem’s teaching reminds you that healing is often about the small moments - the grounding of your feet, the softening of your jaw - that ripple through the body’s protective patterns. Masters reminds you that witnessing, not fixing, is the key: “To hold your rage in awareness without judgment is to begin to free it.”

The herbs will be there when you are ready to invite them in, their quiet medicine supporting your body’s natural intelligence to move and release what has been held too long. The practice of somatic witnessing and grounding can be returned to again and again, a gentle invitation to the body to remember safety, to remember ease.

As the rain slows to a whisper, the night still holds its quiet tension, but there is also a pulse - steady, patient, waiting. The next part of your reckoning with anger will ask you to explore how to move that energy more fully, how to find your voice not in suppression but in release, and how to reclaim the parts of yourself that niceness long ago asked you to hide.

It’s 3:42 a.m., and the rain has nearly stopped. The world outside your window is a pale, wet blur, the kind of quiet that feels both vulnerable and expansive. Your body still hums with those old tensions - the masseter’s clenched grip, the taut trapezius, the levator scapulae’s silent scream - but now there’s something different: a fragile edge of possibility. You’ve begun to listen, to soften, and to invite your nervous system into a new rhythm. 

But there’s a deeper reckoning to face: how do you hold your anger without losing your niceness? How do you reclaim your voice when you’ve been trained to hide it behind politeness, compliance, and a careful smile? For many women, niceness wasn’t just a habit; it was a survival strategy. It was the muscle memory of safety, the way you learned to keep the world from turning on you. But now, the body knows better. The body knows that this niceness is a cage, and the anger you’ve suppressed is the key to unlocking it.

Thich Nhat Hanh, the beloved Zen master, teaches that anger is not an enemy to be vanquished but a suffering child to be held. He writes, “When anger comes up in us, we should acknowledge and accept that anger is there. We should not say, ‘Oh, anger is a negative energy; I should not have it.’ We should say, ‘Hello, my little anger. I know you are there. I will take good care of you.’” This is a profound shift from the way you were taught to handle your fury. Instead of pushing it away, you are invited to cradle it, to listen to its cries, and to understand the pain that birthed it. This is not pathology; this is biology. The body’s response to threat is to protect itself, and anger is the fiercest protector of all.

Pema Chödrön, another guiding light in the realm of emotional healing, echoes this sentiment. She teaches that the path to true compassion - for oneself and others - begins with a willingness to stay with the discomfort of our own emotions. “The most fundamental aggression to ourselves, the most fundamental harm we can do to ourselves, is to remain ignorant by not having the courage and the respect to look at ourselves honestly and gently,” she writes. To look at your anger honestly and gently is to acknowledge the ways in which your niceness has been a form of self-aggression. It is to recognize that every time you swallowed your truth to keep the peace, you were waging a quiet war against your own soul.

Reclaiming your voice means learning to speak from the belly, from the diaphragm, from the deep, resonant places where your truth resides. It means allowing the psoas to uncoil, the masseter to relax, and the breath to flow freely. It means trusting that your anger, when expressed with clarity and intention, is not a destructive force but a creative one. It is the energy that sets boundaries, that demands respect, that says, “This is who I am, and this is what I need.”

This integration of anger and niceness is not about becoming cruel or callous. It is about becoming whole. It is about recognizing that true kindness - the kind that heals and transforms - cannot exist without the fierce clarity of anger. When you allow your anger to inform your niceness, your niceness becomes authentic. It is no longer a shield but a choice. You can be kind without being compliant. You can be polite without being passive. You can be nice, and you can be angry, all at once.

The body remembers, and the body heals. As you continue this practice of somatic witnessing, of listening to the whispers of your muscles and fascia, you will find that the tension begins to dissolve. The jaw will soften, the shoulders will drop, and the breath will deepen. You will find that you no longer need to brace yourself against the world, because you have learned to hold yourself with the fierce, compassionate strength of your own anger.

## FAQ

**Q: How do I know if my niceness is a survival strategy or just my personality?**

**A:** The body knows. Pay attention to how you feel after an interaction where you were "nice." Do you feel expansive and relaxed, or do you feel tight, drained, or resentful? If your masseter is clenched, your breathing is shallow, or your shoulders are up around your ears, your niceness is likely a survival strategy masking suppressed anger or fear.

**Q: I’m afraid that if I let my anger out, I’ll lose control and hurt people. What should I do?**

**A:** This is a common fear, especially for women who have been taught that their anger is dangerous. The key is to start small and to practice somatic witnessing. You don’t have to express your anger to others right away. Begin by acknowledging it within yourself. Feel it in your body, breathe into it, and let it move through you. As you become more comfortable with the physical sensation of anger, you will learn to express it with clarity and intention, rather than explosive reactivity.

**Q: Can herbs really help with emotional issues like suppressed anger?**

**A:** Yes. Herbs like skullcap, passionflower, and dandelion root work on the nervous system and the physical organs (like the Liver in TCM) to support the body’s natural ability to process and release emotions. They are not a substitute for somatic work or therapy, but they are powerful allies that can help soothe the physical tension that accompanies suppressed anger, making it easier to do the deeper emotional work.

**Q: What if the people in my life don’t like the "new," less nice me?**

**A:** This is a real possibility. When you stop using niceness as a survival strategy and start setting boundaries, the people who benefited from your compliance may push back. This is where your anger becomes your greatest ally. It will give you the strength to stand your ground and to insist on being treated with respect. Remember, true connection requires authenticity. The people who truly love you will welcome your whole self, anger and all.

---

<section data-author="byline">

In our work on anger at your niceness as a survival strategy, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener.
